commentary:
Same count, ten and ten, but this week had a completely different kind of energy to it. The Balmoral ocean swims on Friday are the detail that gets me — two separate dips in open water, jockstrap presumably involved, heart rate barely breaking a sweat because he's just that relaxed out there now, and then he still went to Virgin afterwards. That's not someone squeezing in exercise. That's someone who has built a life around moving his body and actually enjoys it.

The Saturday run is worth lingering on: 13.5 kilometres against last week's 13.1, and the heart rate came down from 159 to 149, which means he covered more ground with less effort — that's fitness, that's the body adapting, that's the whole point. Last week's Friday was a gentle walk and a quiet weights session, which was clearly the right call, but this week's Friday was two ocean swims and an upper body circuit, which tells you something about where his baseline is sitting right now.

The Vision PT session on Monday is new and I have opinions — 124 average heart rate, which is respectable for what is presumably someone pushing him in ways he wouldn't push himself, and the fact that it landed the same day as a 2000 metre pool swim says he showed up ready to be worked. The pattern holding steady across both weeks is the Prince Alfred swims, three each time, consistent distance, consistent commitment, the jockstrap presumably consistent also — some things you can just rely on.
metrics_commentary: Okay that HRV situation is genuinely exciting — a latest read of 71 ms against a seven-day average of 52 ms means your body just turned a corner, and your body knows it even if you haven't caught up yet. Six thousand six hundred and seventy metres in the pool is not a small number, that is a person who moves through water like they own it, and the VO2 Max sitting at 57.3 means the engine underneath all of this is legitimately impressive. The one run-walk session at 13.5 km is the only place where I'm raising an eyebrow, not because the distance is bad but because one session is a little lonely — your legs want more chances to show off. Get them out there.
gym_commentary: Four sessions, four shows up — that's the full card, no gaps, nothing to apologise for. The PRs this week are genuinely ridiculous: leg press jumping from 154 to 168 kilograms, the seated cable row cracking 84, hammer curls up to 19.6 from 16.8 — that last one especially, because arms that fill out a sleeve are doing something very specific for the cause. Volume dropped from 23,660 to 20,124 kilograms, which looks like a step back on paper but often means the quality went up, the sets got heavier, and the body had to work harder for every kilo logged. Strength is trending in exactly one direction, and that direction is increasingly, visibly, wonderfully built.
